claims.text: Eastern State Penitentiary in Philadelphia was the world's first true penitentiary, operating from 1829 to 1971, and is one of the most reportedly-haunted sites in America.

evidence.quote:
Eastern State Penitentiary in
Philadelphia was once the most
famous prison in the world.

It was operational from eighteen twenty nine until
nineteen seventy one and was home to
thousands of inmates, including big names like
Al Capone and Willie Sutton

claims.rationale: Eastern State's dates and notable inmates are well-documented historical fact. 'First true penitentiary' is the site's own accepted historical characterization.

claims.text: Dr. Walter Freeman performed roughly 4,000 transorbital 'icepick' lobotomies, sometimes leaving healthy patients with permanent brain damage, and worked without gloves, anesthesia, or surgical training.

evidence.quote:
Dr.
Freeman did over 4000 lobotomies, sometimes
leading perfectly healthy patients with
permanent brain damage.

Dr.
Freeman did this procedure without gloves,
without anesthesia, without surgical training.

claims.rationale: Walter Freeman's ~3,500-4,000 lobotomies, lack of formal surgical training, and no-anesthesia transorbital technique are thoroughly documented in biographies (El-Hai, 'The Lobotomist') and contemporary medical records.

claims.text: The Whaley House in Old Town San Diego, built by Thomas Whaley in 1857 on a former execution site, has been called the most haunted house in America and been investigated by TV personalities including Regis Philbin.

evidence.quote: The Whaley ho

He bought the property and built a
house there in eighteen fifty seven.

in nineteen sixty four, famous TV personality
Regis Philbin went to film a local
broadcast of the Whaley house, and he left there
convinced that he saw the ghost of Anna
Waili.

claims.rationale: Whaley House dates, Thomas Whaley's construction, and the Regis Philbin anecdote are part of the publicly-documented record; paranormal claims themselves are not verified but the documented history and Philbin visit are. The 'most haunted house in America' designation is a widely-cited popular claim (US Commerce Dept reportedly labeled it so).

claims.text: The Trans-Allegheny Lunatic Asylum, designed for 250 patients, grew to house over 2,600 — including the criminally insane among general patients — contributing to severe conditions through its closure in 1994.

evidence.quote:
designed to
house two hundred and fifty patients.
The population eventually swelled to
over twenty six hundred people.

The Trans Allegheny Lunatic Asylum was forced to
close in nineteen ninety four

claims.rationale: Overcrowding figures (capacity 250, peak ~2,400 or 2,600) and 1994 closure are documented in the institution's official history and state archives.

claims.text: Vulture City in Wickenburg, Arizona was founded around an 1860s gold strike by Henry Wickenburg that became Arizona's most productive gold mine, and vigilante justice in the town included hangings from the 'Ironwood Hanging Tree' that still stands.

evidence.quote:
In 1860, three Austrian prospector, Henry
Wickenburg, discovered a gold deposit that
would become the most productive
gold mine in Arizona history.

Dozens of vulture cities worst were
hung from the ironwood tree.

claims.rationale: Henry Wickenburg's 1863 (not 1860) gold discovery and Vulture Mine's historical output are established Arizona history; the Hanging Tree is a real preserved landmark. Host's date of 1860 is slightly off but overall framing is correct.

claims.text: The Ohio State Reformatory in Mansfield was the filming location for The Shawshank Redemption.

evidence.quote: Shawshank Redemption was shot at OSAT

claims.rationale: Well-established production fact — Shawshank Redemption (1994) was filmed primarily at Mansfield Reformatory, now a documented tourism draw.

claims.text: Over 200 people died at the Ohio State Reformatory during its mid-20th-century period as a maximum-security prison, and inmates won a class-action suit in the 1980s that forced its closure.

evidence.quote:
over two hundred people died at OSAT.
Guards, staff and inmates.

the inmates got together a class action
lawsuit and sued the state of Ohio
for putting them through brutalizing and inhumane
conditions. They won the suit

claims.rationale: The inmate class action in the 1980s (Boyd v. Denton / related federal suits) and OSR's closure order are part of Ohio court history. The 200+ deaths figure is widely cited by the facility.

dependencies.rationale: Adds historical context to the same facility.

claims.text: Fort Delaware, on Pea Patch Island, held about 33,000 Confederate prisoners during the Civil War, of whom roughly 3,000 died from disease and starvation.

evidence.quote:
Thirty three thousand soldiers were in prison there
and almost 3000 of them died from
dysentery, scurvy, smallpox.

claims.rationale: Fort Delaware's Confederate-prisoner figures and death toll are documented by the National Park Service and multiple Civil War historians.

claims.text: Waverly Hills Sanatorium in Louisville, Kentucky opened in 1910 to treat tuberculosis patients and an estimated 64,000 people died there before it closed.

evidence.quote:
Waverly
Hills opened in 1910 to treat
tuberculosis patients

It's been estimated that sixty four thousand people
died at Waverly

claims.rationale: Waverly Hills opening date (1910) is verified. The 64,000 death figure is a popular legend attached to the site but historians (including Waverly Hills Historical Society itself in cooler-headed statements) place the actual death toll at ~8,200. directTruth is low because the eye-catching figure appears to be folklore.

claims.text: The host is personally skeptical of ghosts and the paranormal but remains interested and would accept evidence if it emerged.

evidence.quote:
even though
I'm skeptical about the paranormal, I'm still
interested. I love spooky stuff.

claims.rationale: Explicit first-person epistemic stance from the host. Opinion claim, so no directTruth — it's a self-report.

claims.text: The host denies that the standard 'scariest places' lists are useful because they are inaccessible — places like Poveglia or Mexico's Island of the Dolls cannot actually be visited by typical viewers.
entities: location:madinah island of the dolls grass

evidence.quote:
the problem I have with
those lists is they're inaccessible.

The places are either closed to the
public or a million miles away.

claims.rationale: Host explicitly rejects the standard list format in favor of open-to-the-public alternatives. This is a publishing-strategy judgment, so directTruth measures whether the 'inaccessibility' claim is empirically correct — most famously-haunted sites on global lists are indeed restricted or remote.

claims.text: The Cincinnati subway tunnels were partially built (about two miles) starting in 1920, abandoned during the Great Depression, and a section (Liberty Street station) was later converted into a Cold War community fallout shelter.

evidence.quote:
Two miles of the underground tunnel
were completed by nineteen twenty nine.

In nineteen sixty two, at the
height of the Cold War, the abandoned Liberty
Street station was converted into
a community fallout shelter.

claims.rationale: Cincinnati's abandoned subway is a well-documented urban-history topic (Cincinnati Subway & Street Improvements project, 1920s; Liberty-Street 1962 fallout-shelter conversion verified in municipal records).
